---
title: "Approaches"
description: "Ways to build composable infectious disease models with EpiAware, and what we want from all of them."
toc: false
page-layout: full
---

Building a model to inform a decision is rarely one step.
You simulate from the prior, fit to data, check the fit, change an assumption, and fit again.
That workflow is far easier when the model is a single object that can be taken apart and put back together, rather than a chain of separate fits whose outputs are handed along and whose uncertainty is lost on the way.

Fitting everything together keeps the statistics honest, but it usually produces a model built for one problem that nobody can reuse.
Composition is our attempt to keep the joint fit and still be able to swap a part out.
We are not sure yet which way of doing this will work best, so we are trying more than one and keeping them interoperable.

## The approaches

::: {.feature-grid}
::: {.feature-card}
### Composed distributions
Describe what happens to a case over time, from onset through to an outcome, by joining the delays between events into a single distribution.
That one object can simulate a case or be fitted to observed ones, and it can stand in wherever a delay is needed inside a larger model.

[Read the approach &rarr;](composed-distributions.qmd)
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### Composable Turing models
Build a whole model from three interchangeable pieces, one for what drives transmission over time, one for how that produces infections, and one for how infections become the data you actually see.
Any piece can be swapped for another without rewriting the rest.

[Read the approach &rarr;](composable-turing-models.qmd)
:::
:::

More approaches may join these as the ecosystem grows.
Both work with the same underlying packages, and they can be combined in one analysis.

## What we want from any approach

Whatever shape composition takes, we want the same things from it.

- Carry uncertainty the whole way through, from the processes you cannot see to the data you can, and fit everything together rather than in stages.
- Keep the parts of a model separate, and let them nest inside one another, so a part can be reasoned about on its own.
- Support more than one kind of model, whether that is a renewal process, a compartmental model, or something else again.
- Handle populations that differ by age, place, or risk group, without the bookkeeping taking over.
- Let a component carry its own structure and its own priors, so that domain knowledge travels with it.
- Stay useful on its own, so a component earns its keep even outside the wider system.
- Use standard interfaces, so components written independently still fit together and still propagate uncertainty correctly.
- Use one specification for both simulation and inference, so the generative story and the fit cannot drift apart.
- Be quick to change, because assumptions and requirements move fast when a decision is waiting.
